heading: Judicial review of requests for information
label: 3511 Judicial review of requests for information
notes: References in TextSections 626(a), (b) and 627(a) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act, referred to in subsecs. (a), (c), and (d), are classified to sections 1681u(a), (b) and 1681v(a), respectively, of Title 15, Commerce and Trade.Section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act, referred to in subsecs. (a), (c), and (d), probably means section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act of 1978, which is classified to , Banks and Banking.section 3414(a)(5)(A) of Title 12Section 802(a) of the National Security Act of 1947, referred to in subsecs. (a), (c), and (d), is classified to , War and National Defense.section 3162(a) of Title 50

Amendments2015—Subsec. (b). added subsec. (b) and struck out former subsec. (b) which related to petitions for court orders modifying or setting aside a nondisclosure requirement imposed in connection with a request for records, reports, or other information.Pub. L. 114–23

L. 114–23, title VI, § 602(c)129 Stat. 294Mar. 9, 2006June 2, 2015“(a).—Existing ReportsAny report made to a committee of Congress regarding national security letters under , United States Code, section 626(d) or 627(c) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( or 1681v(c)), section 1114(a)(3) or 1114(a)(5)(D) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act [of 1978] ( or 3414(a)(5)(D)), or section 802(b) of the National Security Act of 1947 ( [now ]) shall also be made to the Committees on the Judiciary of the House of Representatives and the Senate.section 2709(c)(1) of title 1815 U.S.C. 1681u(d)12 U.S.C. 3414(a)(3)50 U.S.C. 436(b)50 U.S.C. 3162(b)“(b).—Enhanced Oversight of Fair Credit Reporting Act Counterterrorism National Security Letter[Amended , Commerce and Trade.]section 1681v of Title 15“(c)Report on Requests for National Security Letters.—“(1).—In generalIn April of each year, the Attorney General shall submit to Congress an aggregate report setting forth with respect to the preceding year the total number of requests made by the Department of Justice for information concerning different persons under—“(A), United States Code (to access certain communication service provider records);section 2709 of title 18“(B)section 1114 of the Right to Financial Privacy Act [of 1978] () (to obtain financial institution customer records);12 U.S.C. 3414“(C)section 802 of the National Security Act of 1947 () [now ] (to obtain financial information, records, and consumer reports);50 U.S.C. 43650 U.S.C. 3162“(D)section 626 of the Fair Credit Reporting Act () (to obtain certain financial information and consumer reports); and15 U.S.C. 1681u“(E)section 627 of the Fair Credit Reporting Act () (to obtain credit agency consumer records for counterterrorism investigations).15 U.S.C. 1681v“(2)Content.—“(A).—In generalExcept as provided in subparagraph (B), each report required under this subsection shall include a good faith estimate of the total number of requests described in paragraph (1) requiring disclosure of information concerning—“(i)United States persons; and“(ii)persons who are not United States persons.“(B).—ExceptionWith respect to the number of requests for subscriber information under , United States Code, a report required under this subsection need not separate the number of requests into each of the categories described in subparagraph (A).section 2709 of title 18“(3).—Unclassified formThe report under this section shall be submitted in unclassified form.“(d).—National Security Letter DefinedIn this section, the term ‘national security letter’ means a request for information under one of the following provisions of law:“(1), United States Code (to access certain communication service provider records).Section 2709(a) of title 18“(2)Section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act [of 1978] () (to obtain financial institution customer records).12 U.S.C. 3414(a)(5)(A)“(3)Section 802 of the National Security Act of 1947 () [now ] (to obtain financial information, records, and consumer reports).50 U.S.C. 43650 U.S.C. 3162“(4)Section 626 of the Fair Credit Reporting Act () (to obtain certain financial information and consumer reports).15 U.S.C. 1681u“(5)Section 627 of the Fair Credit Reporting Act () (to obtain credit agency consumer records for counterterrorism investigations).”15 U.S.C. 1681v

children.label: a
children.text: The recipient of a request for records, a report, or other information under , section 626(a) or (b) or 627(a) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act, section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act, or section 802(a) of the National Security Act of 1947 may, in the United States district court for the district in which that person or entity does business or resides, petition for an order modifying or setting aside the request. The court may modify or set aside the request if compliance would be unreasonable, oppressive, or otherwise unlawful.section 2709(b) of this title

children.heading: .—Notice
children.label: A .—Notice
children.text: If a recipient of a request or order for a report, records, or other information under , section 626 or 627 of the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( and 1681v), section 1114 of the Right to Financial Privacy Act of 1978 (), or section 802 of the National Security Act of 1947 (), wishes to have a court review a nondisclosure requirement imposed in connection with the request or order, the recipient may notify the Government or file a petition for judicial review in any court described in subsection (a).section 2709 of this title15 U.S.C. 1681u12 U.S.C. 341450 U.S.C. 3162

children.heading: .—Application
children.label: B .—Application
children.text: Not later than 30 days after the date of receipt of a notification under subparagraph (A), the Government shall apply for an order prohibiting the disclosure of the existence or contents of the relevant request or order. An application under this subparagraph may be filed in the district court of the United States for the judicial district in which the recipient of the order is doing business or in the district court of the United States for any judicial district within which the authorized investigation that is the basis for the request is being conducted. The applicable nondisclosure requirement shall remain in effect during the pendency of proceedings relating to the requirement.

children.heading: .—Consideration
children.label: C .—Consideration
children.text: A district court of the United States that receives a petition under subparagraph (A) or an application under subparagraph (B) should rule expeditiously, and shall, subject to paragraph (3), issue a nondisclosure order that includes conditions appropriate to the circumstances.

children.label: A
children.text: a danger to the national security of the United States;

children.label: B
children.text: interference with a criminal, counterterrorism, or counterintelligence investigation;

children.label: D
children.text: danger to the life or physical safety of any person.

children.label: A
children.text: a danger to the national security of the United States;

children.label: B
children.text: interference with a criminal, counterterrorism, or counterintelligence investigation;

children.label: D
children.text: danger to the life or physical safety of any person.

children.label: c
children.text: In the case of a failure to comply with a request for records, a report, or other information made to any person or entity under , section 626(a) or (b) or 627(a) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act, section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act, or section 802(a) of the National Security Act of 1947, the Attorney General may invoke the aid of any district court of the United States within the jurisdiction in which the investigation is carried on or the person or entity resides, carries on business, or may be found, to compel compliance with the request. The court may issue an order requiring the person or entity to comply with the request. Any failure to obey the order of the court may be punished by the court as contempt thereof. Any process under this section may be served in any judicial district in which the person or entity may be found.section 2709(b) of this title

children.label: d
children.text: In all proceedings under this section, subject to any right to an open hearing in a contempt proceeding, the court must close any hearing to the extent necessary to prevent an unauthorized disclosure of a request for records, a report, or other information made to any person or entity under , section 626(a) or (b) or 627(a) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act, section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act, or section 802(a) of the National Security Act of 1947. Petitions, filings, records, orders, and subpoenas must also be kept under seal to the extent and as long as necessary to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of a request for records, a report, or other information made to any person or entity under , section 626(a) or (b) or 627(a) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act, section 1114(a)(5)(A) of the Right to Financial Privacy Act, or section 802(a) of the National Security Act of 1947.section 2709(b) of this titlesection 2709(b) of this title

children.label: e
children.text: In all proceedings under this section, the court shall, upon request of the government, review ex parte and in camera any government submission or portions thereof, which may include classified information.
